Apart from that, an optimization model can be developed to balance supply and demand for electrical energy. And it can also be used to automate the use of electrical devices in the building.

title: Cobweb: Enhanced Generation Diversity for Black-box Fairness Testing
authors: Yingqian Guo, Wentian Zhao, Tian Song
abstract: Black-box fairness testing aims to reveal potential discriminatory behaviors in deployed AI models by generating individual discriminatory instances, thereby safeguarding trustworthiness in socially critical domains such as smart education and talent recruitment. However, existing approaches often suffer from limited instance space coverage due to their reliance on local neighborhood searches around predefined seed instances, leading to poor diversity and suboptimal exploration. To address these limitations, we propose Cobweb, a novel black-box fairness testing framework that combines genetic algorithms with explicit region-guided generation and multiobjective evolutionary search. First, the initial population is constructed via spatial uniform initialization to maximize instance diversity through pairwise distance optimization. Second, we introduce an explicit region-guided strategy and design a dualobjective optimization mechanism, which concurrently optimizes for both individual discrimination and spatial sparsity, enabling outward search into low-density regions. These mechanisms jointly improve Cobweb’s ability to generate diverse and spatially representative discriminatory instances. Extensive experiments on seven benchmark tabular datasets demonstrate that Cobweb achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with gains in effectiveness ($\sim 1.6 \times$), efficiency ($\sim 2.9 \times$) on average under fixed query budgets. In particular, Cobweb consistently achieves higher scores in three complementary diversity metrics, confirming its superior exploratory capability. Retraining target models with Cobwebgenerated instances leads to an average reduction of 57% in individual fairness violations, confirming the practical value of our approach.

title: Meteor Trail Channel Detection and Classification Based on All-Sky Meteor Radar
authors: Yupeng Li, Zhuangzhuang Tie, Huaitao Xu, Ziyang Wang, Zan Li, Jia Shi
publish: 2025 IEEE 25th International Conference on Communication Technology (ICCT)
abstract: Meteor burst communication (MBC) plays a vital role in emergency scenarios such as natural disasters and military operations, with its performance heavily influenced by meteor trail types — underdense and overdense. This paper presents a framework combining an enhanced detection algorithm and a deep neural network (DNN) for accurate classification of meteor trails using all-sky meteor radar data. Key contributions include a modified signal merging and duration-based screening method that effectively preserves non-specular overdense echoes typically discarded in conventional atmospheric studies, and a simple yet efficient radio frequency interference (RFI) suppression technique based on column-sum thresholding without distorting genuine meteor echoes. A four-layer DNN is designed to extract discriminative features from preprocessed IQ data segments automatically. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves precision and F1-scores exceeding 0.97 on the validation set, significantly improving classification accuracy. This work provides a reliable foundation for modeling MBC channels and optimizing systems.

title: Ghost-DCE: A Lightweight Zero-Reference Low-Light Image Enhancement Method
authors: Youshun Zheng, Lingling Xiong, Sujie Liu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Consumer Electronics and Computer Engineering (ICCECE)
abstract: To address the issues where existing low-light enhancement methods often rely on expensive paired reference data or are difficult to deploy on resource-constrained devices, this paper proposes Ghost-DCE, an improved lightweight zero-reference low-light enhancement method based on Zero-DCE++. First, we design the Ghost-SE module, which integrates large-kernel convolutions with Squeeze-and-Excitation attention. This design significantly expands the effective receptive field and strengthens global illumination context capturing capabilities while maintaining an extremely low parameter count. Second, by introducing a multi-scale spatial consistency loss and a cosine similarity loss, we effectively alleviate the contradiction between noise suppression and detail preservation, achieving a better balance between structural texture retention and color fidelity. Experimental results on the LOL dataset demonstrate that Ghost-DCE achieves highly competitive performance: with only 10.7k parameters, it reaches a PSNR of 17.04 dB, realizing the optimal trade-off between enhancement quality and computational efficiency.

title: Long-Term Motion Modeling for Neural Video Compression
authors: Xin Wang, Weihao Shi, Xiongzhuang Liang, Shujuan Zhang, Junjie Ma, Chuanbo Tang, Dong Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Visual Communications and Image Processing (VCIP)
abstract: Recent advances in end-to-end neural video compression (NVC) have attracted increasing attention due to their remarkable improvement in compression efficiency and adaptability. The accuracy of motion modeling in inter-frame prediction plays a critical role in further boosting compression performance. However, most existing NVC methods still rely on a single reference frame in the motion estimation (ME) stage, while long-term information is mainly exploited only in the motion compensation stage. This limitation hinders the ability to capture complex motion patterns, resulting in sub-optimal prediction accuracy. We propose a Long-Term Motion Modeling framework for Deep Video Compression (LMVC). Specifically, we introduce a ConvLSTM-based Long-Term Motion Modeling module into the ME stage, which leverages historical reconstructed frames to continuously refine the temporal content of reference frames. This design improves the consistency and robustness of motion estimation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed LMVC achieves an average BD-rate reduction of 18.7% compared to the VTM software across multiple public datasets and outperforms state-of-the-art neural video compression approaches, verifying the effectiveness and superiority of our motion modeling strategy.

title: Mitigating Connected PAD Corrosion in Hybrid Bonding
authors: Soon-Aik Chew, Yoichi Tanaka, Sven Dewilde, SungWoo Park, Soheila Bagherilimaei, Harold Philipsen, Joeri De Vos, Steven Deckers, Boyao Zhang, Koji Nakata, Yusuke Okazaki, Nobuko Gan, Hideaki Iino, Ryo Takigawa, Tomas Webers, Gerald Beyer, Zsolt Tokei, Eric Beyne
abstract: As wafer-to-wafer (W2W) hybrid bonding scales to sub- 500 nm pitches, new reliability challenges emerge, particularly related to connected PAD corrosion. This study investigates the mechanisms and mitigation strategies for connected PAD corrosion observed during the chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) and pre-bonding processes. A dedicated test vehicle was designed to evaluate the influence of layout geometry, metal line length, number of connected pads, and process conditions. Experimental results reveal that metal line length is the dominant factor influencing corrosion severity, with longer connections exhibiting deeper Cu recesses. Process sensitivity studies show that acidic CMP slurries and deionized water rinses exacerbate corrosion due to electrochemical reactions and static charge accumulation. In contrast, alkaline slurry and alkaline water (Kurita water) rinses effectively suppress corrosion by neutralizing surface charge and stabilizing the Cu/dielectric interface. A process partition study confirms that connected PAD corrosion initiates only after barrier layer removal. These findings provide critical insights into the electrochemical and charge-induced mechanisms of connected PAD corrosion and offer practical guidelines for process optimization in advanced hybrid bonding applications.

title: Interfacial Electromigration Behavior and Reliability Evaluation in Cu/Ag Sintered Joints
authors: Yun-Chan Kim, Dong-Yurl Yu, Junhyuk Son, Dongjin Byun, Junghwan Bang
abstract: The increasing current densities in miniaturized power electronics have highlighted electromigration (EM) as a critical reliability concern for interconnects. While EM degradation has been extensively studied in conventional solder joints, silver sintered joints-despite their superior thermal and electrical properties-remain underexplored. In this study, we examine the EM behavior of Ag sintered joints formed by bonding laser-processed Ag sheets between dummy silicon chips and bare Cu substrates. The joints were tested under direct current stress at 150° C with current densities exceeding 10 kA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> to replicate high-power module conditions. Microstructural degradation was analyzed using SEM-EDS, focusing on void formation and interfacial delamination. Especially, delamination initiated at the cathode due to electromigration-driven Ag atom depletion and progressed more rapidly under higher current density (12.5 kA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), resulting in early joint failure. In contrast, joints under lower current density (2 kA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) exhibited minimal degradation. Differences in degradation behavior were observed between the cathode and anode, with distinct patterns of void and crack formation. These findings confirm that current level and electron flow direction significantly influence the degradation mechanisms. This work enhances the understanding of EMinduced failures in Ag sintered joints and provides valuable insights for improving interconnect reliability in high-density power electronic applications.

title: Overview for Overlay Metrology Challenges and Solutions in Advanced Packaging
authors: Roie Volkovich, Nimrod Bar-Yaakov, Yoav Grauer
abstract: The semiconductor industry is witnessing rapid advancements in packaging technologies, particularly in the realm of advanced packaging and hybrid bonding. Advanced packaging involves innovative methods to interconnect chips, leading to improved electrical performance, reduced power consumption, and increased functionality. Hybrid bonding, a key aspect of advanced packaging, offers a robust solution for achieving high density interconnections and superior mechanical stability. Overlay metrology measurements play a crucial role in the success of advanced packaging fabrication. Accurate overlay measurements ensure precise alignment of various process steps during packaging fabrication, which is essential for maintaining the integrity and functionality of the final product. This paper delves into the importance of overlay metrology in advanced packaging, discussing the methodologies and technologies employed to achieve high accuracy and robustness in measurements. Furthermore, the paper explores the challenges associated with wafer-to-wafer and die-to-wafer overlay metrology. These challenges include achieving consistent alignment across multiple process steps, managing variations in die thickness, and addressing the complexities of multi-die structures. The introduction to wafer-to-wafer and die-to-wafer overlay metrology provides insights into the current state of the art, the technological hurdles faced, and the ongoing efforts to overcome these obstacles. In conclusion, this paper will summarize the critical aspects of overlay metrology in advanced packaging, offering a comprehensive overview of the techniques, importance, and challenges involved.

title: Towards an MDA-Based Approach for Managing Variability in Microservices
authors: Rostom Mamadji, Farida Kherissi, Toufik Benouhiba
publish: 2025 Fourth International Conference on Theoretical and Applicative Aspects of Computer Science (ICTAACS)
abstract: The microservice architecture represents a new way of designing and implementing software systems. This architectural style focusses on the development of small, independent and loosely coupled services that communicate with each other via precisely defined interfaces. Although this architectural style has several advantages, such as flexibility and the ability to integrate multiple technologies, it brings significant challenges, especially in managing variability due to its level of granularity, the number of possible variants and their combinations.Designing complex microservice architectures requires an organised exploration of architectural alternatives while maintaining functional coherence. In this paper, we propose a model-driven approach (MDA) to manage variability through a transformation from the CIM level (use case diagram) to the PIM level (feature diagram), with use cases serving as the primary artefact for architectural variability. Our contribution is based on creating viable configurations that group use cases into microservices based on functional affinities. The method consists of classifying use cases into two types: Endpoint and Simple Use Case. The configurations are then generated by combining use cases according to their relationships. Our approach is based on a rigorous combinatorial generation that ensures consistency and full traceability from the requirements to the resulting architecture. The configurations resulting from the CIM level are visualised as candidate architectures at the PIM level using a feature diagram.

title: Once-for-All Adaptive Predistortion for Harmonic Cancellation in Wideband HF Power Amplifiers
authors: Zhengxiang Li
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Consumer Electronics and Computer Engineering (ICCECE)
abstract: In HF communication systems, harmonic cancellation for power amplifiers (PA) is challenging due to frequent frequency switching and wide operating ranges. Existing predistortion-based methods require real-time feedback recalibration when signal parameters change, limiting adaptability. This paper presents a novel approach: a neural network (NN)-based harmonic cancellation (HC) model trained once with iterative carrier injection to build a universal predistortion model covering the entire HF band and amplitude range. By learning from sparse, discrete training data, the model eliminates the need for feedback-driven recalibration during dynamic signal changes, enabling broad generalization to arbitrary signal characteristics across the HF spectrum. The proposed method establishes a one-time, fully adaptive HC-NN framework that dynamically cancels harmonics without retraining or real-time parameter updates. Experimental results with a 10W HF-PA show consistent harmonic suppression below -70 dBc for all signals in the HF band, verifying robust, calibration-free adaptability to frequency and amplitude variations.

title: Adaptive Edge Intelligence for Bandwidth Optimization in LoRaWAN Gateways
authors: Venkata S V G Sarma Lanka, Kothandaraman D.
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) networks using LoRaWAN has intensified the need for bandwidthefficient and intelligent edge analytics. This paper presents an Adaptive Edge-Intelligence Framework for Time-Windowed Statistical Aggregation (TWSA) that dynamically balances bandwidth, fidelity, and latency for gateway-based anomaly detection. The system integrates a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda$</tex>-controlled windowing mechanism with hybrid statistical (Z-Score) and machine-learning (Isolation Forest) detectors. Experiments across two datasets show compression gains of up to 58%, minimal RSSI distortion <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(<1.6 \%)$</tex>, and real-time inference below 300 ms. Detector performance is data-dependent—machine-learning models excel in structured traffic, whereas statistical detectors remain robust in noisy regimes. These results confirm TWSA as a scalable, context-adaptive, and resource-efficient edge framework for next-generation LoRaWAN gateways.

title: Parameter-Efficient Adaptation of Vision–Language Models for Free-Hand Sketch Recognition
authors: Yifan Zhang, Lan Yang, Kaiyue Pang, Honggang Zhang, Yi-Zhe Song
publish: 2025 International Conference on Visual Communications and Image Processing (VCIP)
abstract: How to prompt a foundation model like CLIP towards a sketch expert is the question we seek to answer in this paper. Debates on the best way to prompt have been intense and divided, however converged on one particular point that of modelling prompt learning as context token optimisation. This paper scrutinises such technical route for sketch and argues the challenge is more than a stereotyped ask from context change. In particular, we pin down the problem to the dramatic cross-modality gap between sketch and the photo-centric visual world formed within CLIP. We first show through a pilot study that relocating a sketched object to a different spatial locality can significantly improve zero-shot CLIP performance on sketch. Our core contribution is then to regard spatial misalignment as the key to explaining poor sketch adaptation in CLIP prompts – that a sketched object does not reside in a place as if it were part of the scene compositions of photo. Methodologically, we leverage a lightweight network that explicitly allows differentiable spatial manipulation of sketch data and design regulatory self-supervised signals to encourage proper convergence. We showcase consistent complementary power of this simple approach by building on top of 10 existing contemporary prompting methods on the sketch recognition task. For example, we outperform the strong prompting baseline CoOp by 2.57%, MaPle by 4.83% and AdaptFormer by 5.07%. Notably, the latter two beat the traditional full parameter fine-tuning (82.98%83.39% vs. 81.51%), and does so with less than 1% of the total training parameters.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Language Models: Evolution, Applications, and Future Directions
authors: Muhammad Aamir, Ziaur Rahman, Uzair Aslam Bhatti, Jameel Ahmed Bhutto, Nomica Choudhry, Waheed Ahmed Abro
publish: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Computer, Vision and Intelligent Technology (ICCVIT)
abstract: Language models have fundamentally transformed natural language processing (NLP) and artificial intelligence (AI) over the past decade. This comprehensive review examines their evolution from rule-based systems to transformer-based architectures, highlighting major breakthroughs and diverse applications. We analyze the transition to large-scale pretrained models and the emergence of multimodal and multilingual capabilities. Beyond technological advances, we assess their impact across healthcare, finance, and education. However, significant challenges remain, including computational costs, ethical concerns, and generalization limitations. We identify future research directions focusing on efficiency, interpretability, and bias mitigation. This review serves as a vital resource for researchers and practitioners, providing insights into current advances and future possibilities in language modeling.

title: MTL-CR: A Multitask Learning Approach for Code Representation
authors: Dongxu Yu, Feiqiao Mao, Xingyang Du, Yifan Bai
abstract: Code representation plays a fundamental role in enabling a wide range of code intelligence tasks, such as code translation, bug fixing, and code completion. Existing approaches typically adopt task-specific modeling paradigms, training separate models for individual downstream tasks. However, such methods often fail to capture semantic and structural commonalities across tasks, resulting in limited generalization and transferability. Therefore, we propose MTL-CR (Multitask Learning for Code Representation), a unified multitask learning framework that jointly optimizes multiple code-related tasks to learn more generalizable and robust code representations. To balance optimization dynamics among tasks, we further introduce a dynamic task weighting strategy based on taskspecific learning speeds. Experimental results demonstrate that MTL-CR consistently outperforms single task baselines and static multitask methods in various downstream tasks and evaluation metrics. For example, in the code translation task, it improves Accuracy and BLEU by 6.29% and 2.33%.

title: Recovering Variable Names in the Decompiled Code Based on Multi-Task Learning
authors: Jiayi Li, Jingxuan Zhang, Jian He, Jingjing Gu, Weiqin Zou
abstract: Decompilation is one of the key techniques in software reverse engineering and is widely used in security-related tasks such as malware analysis and vulnerability detection. Software is typically released in binary form with symbol information stripped. Although decompilers are capable of reconstructing a lot of the information lost during compilation, they often fail to recover meaningful variable names. As a result, the readability of the decompiled code is significantly reduced, severely hindering the efficiency of reverse analysis. To address this problem, we propose a novel variable name recovery approach ReDevar for the decompiled code based on Multi-task Learning (MTL), which takes variable name recovery as the main task and introduces two additional tasks, including data source prediction and name complexity prediction, corresponding to two aspects of semantic understanding and semantic transformation of variables in the decompiled code. Data source prediction simulates the data flow relationship among variable instances, assisting ReDevar better understand the semantics of variables. The name complexity prediction task enables ReDevar to perceive the complexity of the expected variable name at a mask position, obtaining appropriate semantic transformation results. They are both jointly trained with the variable name recovery task to improve the understanding of ReDevar for variable semantics and name composition. We conducted a series of experiments to validate the effectiveness of ReDevar. Experimental results show that ReDevar achieves top- 1 accuracy of $54.44 \%$ on the VarCorpus benchmark dataset, 2.81% and 1.50% higher than the state-of-the-art approaches VarBERT and Resym respectively. In addition, we also evaluated the performance of ReDevar under various conditions, including different dataset splitting strategies, different decompilers, and different optimization levels. The results demonstrate that ReDevar generalizes well across all settings. Furthermore, the ablation study indicates that the two auxiliary tasks we introduced in ReDevar are both beneficial for the variable name recovery task.

title: Guest Editorial Special Feature on Quantum Biology: Series III
authors: Harun Šiljak
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications

title: Investigating Cellular Magnetic Bioeffects Using Two-Channel, Two-Photon Autofluorescence Lifetime Microscopy_supp1-3666858.docx
authors: Stephen A. Boppart
abstract: Magnetic fields may modulate the cellular reduction-oxidation (redox) state and subsequent redox signaling pathways through quantum spin chemistry in biochemical radical pair reactions. This study applied two-channel, two-photon autofluorescence lifetime microscopy for non-invasive label-free measurements of the metabolic cofactors FAD and NAD(P)H and the redox state in live human non-small lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells. This custom microscope and technique were used to investigate the cellular effects induced by 30-minute exposure to redox modulating chemicals as well as 72-hour exposure to a range of static magnetic fields between 50 μT and 10 mT. The label-free methods showed little sensitivity to the acute chemical exposure in A549 cells, while longer-term magnetic field exposure showed a potential yet non-significant increase in an oxidative stress-linked long lifetime species. A standard H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> assay used to validate these responses showed significant sensitivity to chemical redox modulation reactions, and a weak response to effects caused by magnetic fields. Cellular H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> was observed to increase then gradually saturate as magnetic field exposure increased. These results demonstrate the potential of label-free microscopy for studying subtle magnetic bioeffects in individual living cells.

title: Planarization of Cu/Polymer Surfaces via Fly Cutting: Influence of Polymer Properties and Processing Parameters
authors: Suin Jang, Hoogwan Lee, Sarah Eunkyung Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: As the demand for fine-pitch and high-density interconnects continues to increase, hybrid bonding has emerged as a promising technology to replace conventional 3D integration techniques. While chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) is widely used for Cu/dielectric structure planarization, it has limitations when applied to polymer materials due to surface damage and material loss caused by polymers' low hardness and high ductility. In this study, we propose fly cutting as an alternative to CMP—a mechanical, slurry-free planarization process—and evaluate its applicability to Cu/polymer structures. We selected rigid polyimide (PI) and soft polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as representative polymers to compare degree of planarization (DoP), surface roughness, and bonding performance. Cu/PI structures achieved a high degree of planarization up to 99% and low Cu surface roughness below 2 nm, regardless of spindle conditions. In contrast, Cu/PDMS structures achieved only 67% planarization due to PDMS deformation during fly cutting, leaving the PDMS surface lower than the Cu layer. This facilitates earlier Cu–Cu contact during bonding. ATR-FTIR analysis elucidated the surface activation mechanism through O₂ plasma treatment, clarifying the polymer–polymer bonding process through functional group changes. Post-bonding FE-SEM evaluation confirmed polymer squeeze behavior and changes in upper and lower Cu pad spacing according to temperature and spindle conditions, along with successful polymer bonding. Fly cutting showed promise as a low-cost polymer planarization method, though further optimization of the polymer and process conditions is needed.

title: Integrated Primary-Secondary Distribution System State Estimation With Bimodal Error Distribution
authors: Surendra Bajagain, Anamika Dubey
abstract: Traditionally, measurement errors are assumed to follow Gaussian distribution in the state estimation process. However, statistical analysis of field measurements has shown that measurement errors can have multimodal and skewed distribution. This work aims to develop a static power distribution system state estimator that can capture skewed bimodal measurement error distribution and enable a joint medium-voltage (MV) primary feeders and low-voltage (LV) secondary feeders state estimation. A distributed primary-secondary (MV-LV) state estimation algorithm is proposed and iteratively executed until convergence at the MV-LV boundary. The primary state estimator is formulated as an equality-constrained maximum likelihood estimation problem and the secondary state estimator is formulated as a forward-backward sweep power flow problem. The proposed algorithm is demonstrated using the IEEE 123 bus distribution test feeder modified to include secondary network extensions at 47 different nodes. The simulation results show that the state estimation results are improved by considering skewed bimodal distribution for measurement errors, the performance of the state estimator with the approximate secondary model is highly sensitive to unbalanced loadings of the split-phase customer and the convergence speed of the joint MV-LV state estimation process is improved by collecting all the legs active and reactive power demands of the split-phase customer in LV feeders.

title: EQA-MDL: Wearable ECG Signal Quality Assessment via Multi-scale Difference Learning
authors: Haoyi Fan, Jiawei Luo, Huihui Chang, Han Han, Jian Tan, Chunyi Guo, Muwei Li, Zongmin Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Signal quality assessment (SQA) is crucial in data collection and analysis based on wearable devices. However, most existing electrocardiogram (ECG) quality assessment methods rely on labeled data and struggle to accurately locate noisy areas. To address this issue, we propose a self-supervised method based on multi-scale difference learning, called EQA-MDL, which utilizes a noise generation module to provide pseudo-anomaly samples for difference learning and combines a reconstruction framework with multi-scale difference learning to capture key features for accurate anomaly localization. Specifically, we first design an ECG noise generation method to simulate real-world noise scenarios, thereby generating corresponding pseudo-anomalous samples along with their pixel-level labels. Second, a reconstruction-based self-supervised framework is introduced that leverages pseudo-anomalies to adversarially learn optimal latent feature representations of clean ECG signals. Finally, we design a multi-scale difference learning function to amplify the representational differences between noisy and high-quality ECG features, while incorporating representational correlations from the observation space into the feature space, thereby extracting more robust features. Extensive experiments conducted on two public datasets and three private datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Impact of Sunglasses on One-to-Many Facial Identification Accuracy_supp1-3666896.pdf
authors: Kevin W. Bowyer
abstract: Probe images used in one-to-many facial identification often deviate from ‘mugshot quality’ in various ways. This paper explores how the accuracy of one-to-many facial identification is degraded by the person in the probe image wearing sunglasses, and how this accuracy degradation can be ameliorated. We show that sunglasses degrade accuracy for mugshot-quality images by an amount similar to strong blur or noticeably lower resolution. Further, we demonstrate that the combination of sunglasses with blur or lower resolution has an additive effect in degrading accuracy. These results have important implications for developing criteria to qualify a probe image for the accuracy that can be expected if it is used for facial identification. We explore two approaches to reduce the accuracy loss. First, we show that by adding synthetic sunglasses to all gallery images, we can recover approximately 27% of the accuracy loss with no change to the face-matching model. Second, we demonstrate that training a sunglasses-aware model using augmented training data can achieve up to 33% higher accuracy for sunglasses-wearing probes while maintaining baseline accuracy for non-sunglasses probes. Moreover, combining both approaches achieves even greater accuracy recovery (up to 43% for males and 35% for females). Additionally, our results show that increasing the representation of sunglasses-wearing images evenly across all identities in training data is more effective than concentrating them on fewer identities. The effectiveness of our sunglasses-aware model is validated through t-SNE visualization and a 16% increase in feature similarity between sunglasses and no-sunglasses images of a given identity. Our investigation further reveals that baseball caps play a role similar to sunglasses in degrading facial identification accuracy. To support replication and further research, info to obtain the face image datasets used is available at https://cvrl.nd.edu/projects/data/.

title: LiDAR Beacon: 300 m-Range Fiducial Marker for Mobile Robot Localization with Dual Temporal Range–Reflectance Modulation
authors: Ozora Sako, Kenji Koide, Rokuto Nagata, Kazuma Ikeda, Ryo Yoshida, Kentaro Yoshioka
abstract: Autonomous mobile robots require accurate localization in large-scale environments where Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signals are unavailable. Mobile LiDAR-detectable fiducial markers can provide absolute position references, but existing approaches encode identifiers (IDs) using static geometric/reflectance patterns, which limits detection range due to LiDAR resolution. We introduce LiDAR Beacon, a novel mobile LiDAR-based fiducial marker that achieves reliable long-range detection and identification. LiDAR Beacon encodes its ID by introducing controlled modulations in both the range and reflectance of LiDAR returns via an electrically switchable polymer-dispersed liquid crystal (PDLC) layer. This approach enables robust ID extraction using only a single-point LiDAR return tracked over several scan frames. To address different deployment needs, we introduce two operating modes prioritizing either decoding speed or installation flexibility. Experimental validation demonstrates exceptional performance, achieving a maximum outdoor detection range of ≈ 309m with a precision of 100.0% and a recall of 29.7% under targeted conditions. Furthermore, we demonstrate the system’s practical utility by integrating infrastructure-mounted LiDAR Beacons as absolute references into a factor-graph-based multi-sensor localization framework. Field experiments in large-scale outdoor and indoor environments show that our system significantly reduces marker density requirements while maintaining high localization accuracy, even when conventional LiDAR scan matching fails or only sparse markers are available.

title: Machine Learning Models Predicting Radiation Pneumonitis Based on a Multi-regional Radiomics and Dosiomics Approach in Patients With Lung Cancer Treated on the NRG/RTOG 0617 Clinical Trial_supp2-3663985.xlsx
authors: Taman Upadhaya
abstract: We explored a multi-regional imaging-omics approach to extract computed tomography (CT)-based radiomics and 3-dimenional (3D) dosiomics features to predict radiation pneumonitis (RP) in patients with locally advanced non-small cell lung cancer (LA-NSCLC). This study analyzed 451 patients from the NRG Oncology/Radiation Therapy Oncology Group (RTOG) 0617 clinical trial data set. A radiomics analysis pipeline was implemented to extract features from CT and 3D dose maps using multi-regional lung contours (clinical target volume; ipsilateral, contralateral and total lungs) to predict grade≥2 RP. Predictive models, generalized linear model with LASSO penalty (LASSO-GLM), Random Forest (RF), XGBoost, and Support Vector Machine (linear and RBF) were evaluated. The model combining radiomics with dosiomics-based features from total lungs achieved the highest area under the curve (AUC, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.65-0.83) on the validation dataset and 0.70 (95% CI, 0.56-0.83) on the test dataset using SVM-RBF algorithm. Combining radiomics, dosiomics, and filter-based features from the contralateral lung both achieved AUC of 0.69 in the test dataset using the SVM-RBF algorithm, similar to dosiomics features extracted from total lungs. These results are suggestive that radiomics, dosiomics, and filter features on radiotherapy planning CT scans and total lung 3D dose distributions can provide complimentary information to enhance model performance and inform signatures for prediction of RP.

title: A Gravity Matching Adaptability Analysis Method based on Gravity Directional Characteristics
authors: Wenzhe Zhang, Yu Wang, Shengwu Zhao, Zhihong Deng
abstract: Gravity aided inertial navigation system is one of the key technologies for underwater navigation and positioning. The position accuracy relates to the spatial variability and directional characteristics of the gravity anomaly map. To quantify the directional adaptability of gravity anomaly maps, this paper proposes a novel analysis method based on gravity directional characteristics. Firstly, three key parameters are designed: gravity profile similarity, directional gravity gradient, and directional gradient concentration, which characterize the directional distribution of gravity anomalies. Then, a balanced gravity characteristic parameter is constructed by fusing classical gravity metrics. The mapping between these features and directional matching rates is then modeled using an LM-BP neural network, establishing an adaptability evaluation framework. Experimental results demonstrate that in regions with directional adaptability exceeding 0.8, the matching rates are over 80%. The proposed model offers higher accuracy and comprehensiveness in selecting gravity matching areas than existing methods and provides a practical tool for optimizing gravity matching accuracy.

title: Classical and Sliding Mode Controllers Applied to the Reaction Wheel Inverted Pendulum_supp1-3667087.mp4
authors: RAFAEL MAXIMO DA SILVA

title: Enhancement-Mode Lattice-Matched AlInN/GaN/AlGaN/GaN Heterostructured-Fin-Gated Multichannel and Double-Channel MOSHEMTs
authors: Hong-You Chen, Yu-Lun Lyu, Ching-Ting Lee, Hsin-Ying Lee
abstract: To simultaneously achieve high sheet electron density and high electron mobility, vertically laminated lattice-matched AlInN/GaN/AlGaN/GaN heterostructured double two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) channels were grown on silicon substrates. Using the epitaxial layers and the advantage of high gate control capability, various wide fin-gated multichannel structures were manufactured. When ferroelectric stacked LiNbO3/HfO2/Al2O3 films were utilized as gate oxide layers, enhancement-mode fin-gated multichannel and double-2DEG channels metal-oxide-semiconductor high-electron mobility transistors (MOSHEMTs) were fabricated. Compared the planar devices with the 80-nm-wide multichannel devices, the normalized drain-source current increased from 273.7 mA/mm to 701.3 mA/mm, the subthreshold swing improved from 218 mV/dec to 96 mV/dec, the maximum transconductance improved from 100.8 mS/mm to 270.3 mS/mm, and the threshold voltage shifted from 2.1 V toward 2.5 V. By utilizing the fin-gated multichannel structure, not only could the double-hump transconductance characteristics of planar devices be transferred into smooth single-hump transconductance characteristics, but low-frequency noise power density could also be reduced and high-frequency characteristics improved. Furthermore, the fin-gated multichannel devices with narrower channel width exhibited better characteristics.

title: ARGfore: A multivariate framework for forecasting antibiotic resistance gene abundances using time-series metagenomic datasets_supp1-3667074.pdf
authors: Liqing Zhang

title: EpidemIQs: Prompt-to-Paper LLM Agents for Epidemic Modeling and Analysis_supp1-3666830.pdf
authors: Mohammad Hossein Samaei
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new opportunities to accelerate complex interdisciplinary research domains through AI assistant tools. Epidemic modeling, characterized by its complexity and reliance on network science, dynamical systems, epidemiology, and stochastic simulations, represents a prime candidate for leveraging LLM-driven automation. We introduce EpidemIQs, a novel multi-agent LLM framework that integrates user inputs and autonomously conducts literature review, analytical derivation, network modeling, mechanistic modeling, stochastic simulations, data visualization and analysis, and finally documentation of findings in a structured manuscript, through five predefined research phases. We introduce two types of agents: a scientist agent for planning, coordination, reflection, and generation of final results, and a task-expert agent to focus exclusively on one specific duty serving as a tool to the scientist agent. The framework consistently generated complete reports in scientific article format. Specifically, using GPT 4.1 and GPT 4.1 Mini as backbone LLMs for scientist and task-expert agents, respectively, the autonomous process completes with average total token usage 870K at a cost of about $1.57 per study, successfully executing all phases and final report. We evaluate EpidemIQs across several different epidemic scenarios, measuring computational cost, workflow reliability, task success rate, and LLM-as-Judge and human expert reviews to estimate the overall quality and technical correctness of the generated results. Through our experiments, the framework consistently addresses evaluation scenarios with an average task success rate of 79%. We compare EpidemIQs to an iterative single-agent LLM, benefiting from the same system prompts and tools, iteratively planning, invoking tools, and revising outputs until task completion. The qualitative and quantitative comparisons suggest a consistently higher performance of the proposed framework across the tested scenarios. We discuss the benefits that EpidemIQs can offer, its limitations, and ethical concerns of such AI research assistant tools.

title: Leader-following Consensus over Jointly Connected Switching Networks is Achievable for Exponentially Unstable Linear Systems
authors: Yuhan Chen, Tao Liu, Jie Huang
abstract: The leader-following consensus problem for general linear multi-agent systems over jointly connected switching networks has been a challenging problem and the solvability of the problem has been limited to the class of linear multi-agent systems whose system matrix is marginally stable. This condition is restrictive since it even excludes the most commonly used double-integrator system. This paper presents an advancement by showing that leader-following exponential consensus is achievable for general linear multi-agent systems over jointly connected switching networks, even when the system matrix is exponentially unstable. The degree of instability can be explicitly characterized by two key quantities that arise from the jointly connected condition on a switching graph. By exploiting duality, we further show that the output-based distributed observer design problem for a general leader system is solvable over jointly connected switching networks, even when the system matrix is exponentially unstable. This is also in sharp contrast to the existing distributed observers, which rely on the assumption that the leader system is marginally stable.

title: Optimizing Demand Response in Virtual Power Plants: A Two-Stage Game-Programming Model
authors: Lu Zhang, Bai-Chen Xie, Peng Hao, Na Duan
abstract: As an emerging intermediary between the supply and demand sides, virtual power plants (VPPs) aggregate and dispatch distributed resources such as energy storage systems and electric vehicles (EVs), facilitating efficient and flexible energy engineering management. Demand response (DR) is an effective means for demand-side management in VPPs. To further optimize the DR strategy, this study introduced a two stage game-programming model for the VPP that considers multiple uncertainties. In the day-ahead stage, the Stackelberg game is used to model hierarchical interactions among the main grid, the VPP operator, and end users. We proposed an integrated DR strategy combining both real-time pricing and dynamic subsidy at this stage. In the intra-day stage, a nonlinear programming method is applied to optimize the charging and discharging of energy storage systems, compensating for deviations between predicted and actual photovoltaic generation. We investigated demand response behaviors across users with differentiated electricity consumption preferences, considering the key role of EVs with vehicle-to-grid (V2G) capabilities in the VPP. To ensure research rigor, this study conducts a case study based on real-world data and validates the robustness of findings through multi-scenario comparisons and sensitivity analyses. Simulation results show that: (i) the introduction of the Vehicle to-Grid business model into the VPP can improve overall stakeholder benefits by 7.6%; (ii) to maximize customers' DR potential, incentive strategies should vary depending on their utility sensitivity and price sensitivity; and (iii) the proposed integrated DR strategy can simultaneously improve VPP operational economics, enhance user satisfaction, and strengthen grid stability.

title: From Bayesian Asymptotics to General Large-Scale MIMO Capacity
authors: Sheng Yang, Richard Combes
abstract: We present a unifying framework that bridges Bayesian asymptotics and information theory to analyze the asymptotic Shannon capacity of general large-scale MIMO channels including ones with nonlinearities or imperfect hardware.We derive both an analytic capacity formula and an asymptotically optimal input distribution in the large-antenna regime, each of which depends solely on the single-output channel’s Fisher information through a term we call the (tilted) Jeffreys factor. We demonstrate how our method applies broadly to scenarios with clipping, coarse quantization (including 1-bit ADCs), phase noise, fading with imperfect CSI, and even optical Poisson channels. Our asymptotic analysis motivates a practical approach to constellation design via a compander-like transformation. Furthermore, we introduce a low-complexity receiver structure that approximates the log-likelihood by quantizing the channel outputs into finitely many bins, enabling near-capacity performance with computational complexity independent of the output dimension. Numerical results confirm that the proposed method unifies and simplifies many previously intractable MIMO capacity problems and reveals how the Fisher information alone governs the channel’s asymptotic behavior.

title: Semantic-Spatial Feature Refinement Network for Road Extraction from Remote Sensing Images_supp1-3665299.pdf
authors: Qi Wang
abstract: Extracting precise road information from remote sensing images remains challenging due to the interference from similar objects and occlusion from surroundings. To alleviate these issues, we propose a novel road extraction network to enhance both the precision and topological connectivity of extracted road networks, dubbed as CRNet. Specifically, a Global-Local Context Decoupling Module (GLCDM) is introduced to explicitly model long-range contextual dependencies while preserving fine-grained local road features, thereby improving the model’s inference capability in occluded regions. Furthermore, a Semantic-Spatial Feature Refinement Module (SSFRM) is integrated into the skip connections, which leverages deep semantic features to guide the suppression of background noise in shallow feature maps across both channel and spatial dimensions, ensuring the decoder receives structurally accurate road representations. Experimental results on the remote sensing road datasets demonstrate that CRNet achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of both segmentation accuracy and road connectivity. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/CVer- Yang/CRNet.

title: Towards the Spectral bias Alleviation by Normalizations in Coordinate Networks_supp1-3667002.pdf
authors: Hao Zhu
abstract: Representing signals using coordinate networks dominates the area of inverse problems recently, and is widely applied in various scientific computing tasks. Still, there exists an issue of spectral bias in coordinate networks, limiting the capacity to learn high-frequency components. This problem is caused by the pathological distribution of the neural tangent kernel's (NTK's) eigenvalues of coordinate networks. We find that, this pathological distribution could be improved using classical normalization techniques (batch normalization and layer normalization), which are commonly used in convolutional neural networks but rarely used in coordinate networks. We prove that normalization techniques greatly reduces the maximum and variance of NTK's eigenvalues while slightly modifies the mean value, considering the max eigenvalue is much larger than the most, this variance change results in a shift of eigenvalues' distribution from a lower one to a higher one, therefore the spectral bias could be alleviated (see Fig. 1). Furthermore, we propose two new normalization techniques by combining these two techniques in different ways. The efficacy of these normalization techniques is substantiated by the significant improvements and new state-of-the-arts achieved by applying normalization-based coordinate networks to various tasks, including the image compression, computed tomography reconstruction, shape representation, magnetic resonance imaging, novel view synthesis and multi-view stereo reconstruction.

title: TraceHG: An Unsupervised Dual-View Framework for Microservice Anomaly Detection
authors: Ningning Han, Siyang Lu, Zaichao Lin, Bin Li, Nan Wang, Xin Luo
abstract: Traces and logs record the behaviors and interactions between microservices, making them indispensable for diagnosing system anomalies. However, the intricate and flexible architecture of microservices presents significant challenges for automated anomaly detection. Existing approaches often struggle to capture the internal dynamics and correlations among microservices, which limits their ability to comprehensively detect anomalies arising from synchronous or asynchronous calls. Therefore, we categorize three prevalent anomaly paradigms in microservice systems: intra-service, inter-service, and joint anomalies. To address these challenges, we propose a novel unsupervised learning framework for dual-view anomaly detection, named TraceHG. Specifically, it constructs the trace graph composed of logs, response times, and traces. Besides, TraceHG utilizes hypergraph transformation to establish two views, providing comprehensive information in microservices. Furthermore, we introduce a dual-view framework that leverages both a hypergraph-view and a graph-view encoders to explicitly learn associations among microservices. These two views, employing graph and HGNNs, capture representations of internal dynamics, invocations, and their associated contexts in microservices. By constructing two minimized hyperspheres and measuring the distances from their centers in the latent space, we identify anomalies based on the anomaly scores. Extensive experiments on public benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in microservice anomaly detection.

title: Revisiting Wireless-Powered MEC: A Cooperative Energy Recycling Framework for Task-Energy Co-Design
authors: Haohao Qin, Bowen Gu, Xianhua Yu, Hao Xie, Yongjun Xu, Qihao Li, Liejun Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 25th International Conference on Communication Technology (ICCT)
abstract: Cooperative energy recycling (CER) offers a new way to boost energy utilization in wireless-powered multi-access edge computing (MEC) networks, yet its integration with computation–communication co-design remains underexplored. This paper proposes a CER-enabled MEC framework that maximizes the minimum computable data among users under energy causality, latency, and power constraints. The intractable problem is reformulated into a convex form through relaxation, maximum ratio combining, and variable substitution, and closed-form solutions are derived via Lagrangian duality and alternating optimization, offering analytical insights. Simulation results verify that the proposed CER mechanism markedly increases total computable data while maintaining equitable performance across heterogeneous users.

title: MSWT: Multi-Scale Wavelet Transformer for Dynamic Feature-Based Student Program Error Identification
authors: Lingzhong Yu, Qing Zhou, Subo Zhao, Siyuan Wang
abstract: Automatic identification and classification of student programming errors represents a fundamental challenge in intelligent programming education. Student code exhibits unique error patterns that differ significantly from professional code, with logic and algorithmic errors being particularly challenging due to their concealment and diverse manifestations. Traditional static analysis methods struggle with complex semantic errors, while existing deep learning approaches lack multi-scale modeling capabilities for program execution dynamics. To address these limitations, we propose a Multi-Scale Wavelet Transformer (MSWT) model that integrates wavelet transforms with deep learning techniques. The model comprises four core modules: a wavelet transform feature enhancement module for multi-scale time-frequency analysis, a parallel CNN-BiLSTM encoder for capturing local patterns and long-term dependencies, an adaptive weighting module for fusing static and dynamic features, and a multi-head self-attention classifier for final prediction. Experiments on 7,007 real code samples from 198 students demonstrate that MSWT achieves 83.91% weighted F1-score on 10-class error classification, out-performing the best baseline by 5.72 percentage points. This work is the first to introduce wavelet transforms into program error analysis and provides interpretable error localization for programming education.

title: Autism Spectrum Disorder Analyzer Using Deep Learning Models
authors: Sarala Pottangi, Sri Vani Mahalakshmi Nellipudi, Bharath Kumar Silla, Vamsi Krishna Dandasi, Satish Muppidi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a complex neurodevelopmental condition that requires early and accurate detection for effective intervention. This project aims to develop a multimodal, explainable deep learning system for early detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). Unlike existing MRI-only methods such as FTRSO-SqueezeNet, the proposed approach integrates brain MRI, facial expressions, and behavioral questionnaire responses. MRI images are preprocessed using U-Net segmentation, features are extracted via EfficientNet, MediaPipe, and BERT, and fused through an attention-based layer. A neural network classifier optimized with Bayesian tuning provides predictions, supported by GradCAM and SHAP for interpretability. The objective is to improve diagnostic accuracy, transparency, and real-time applicability, offering a scalable solution for ASD screening in clinical.

title: Rigid Structure Perturbation for Adversarial 3D Point Clouds Attack
authors: Zhiyuan Ma, Ju Wang, Jun Wang, Xin Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Computer, Vision and Intelligent Technology (ICCVIT)
abstract: Recent research has revealed that deep neural networks (DNNs) are highly susceptible to adversarial examples, where deliberately designed perturbations can cause incorrect model predictions. Although adversarial attacks and corresponding defense strategies have been widely explored in the context of 2D images and conventional machine learning, investigations into these issues for 3D point cloud data are still relatively scarce. Given the critical applications of 3D point cloud perception in autonomous driving and robotics, this paper investigates the impact of adversarial point clouds on existing point cloud classification models. Existing methods often produce adversarial samples containing conspicuous outlier points, which can be easily mitigated by simple outlier removal strategies. However, human perception mechanisms differ significantly between 2D images and 3D structures. In this work, we present an innovative framework that utilizes object rigidity as a structural prior to generate adversarial point clouds against the popular deep learning model PointNet++. Our approach perturbs points along surface normals based on the geometric properties of objects, preserving their structural features while ensuring surface smoothness and naturalness, thereby generating adversarial attacks that are imperceptible to humans. Based on this design, we introduce a simple yet effective algorithm, Rigid Structure Perturbation for Adversarial 3D Point Clouds (RSP-3DAdv). Extensive experiments on the ModelNet40 3D shape classification dataset demonstrate that RSP-3DAdv achieves superior attack success rates while maintaining the structural integrity of object surfaces.

title: Enhancing the Efficiency of Answering High-dimensional Why-not kNN Questions
authors: Qi Cao, Fei Ye, Lu Jia, Bin Huang, Zhenying He, Yinan Jing, Kai Zhang, X.Sean Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Big Data and Smart Computing (BigComp)
abstract: The why-not problem explains why expected results are missing from query outputs, helping users understand and refine data exploration. While k-Nearest Neighbor (kNN) queries are fundamental to similarity search and widely used in recommendation and retrieval, existing why-not methods—mostly designed for relational queries—struggle with high-dimensional, large-scale kNN settings. To address this, we propose ipDistance, an efficient indexing structure based on polar coordinates and distance pruning to accelerate kNN search and prune the search space. We further design a high-dimensional why-not kNN explanation algorithm that balances accuracy and efficiency. Experiments on two real-world datasets show our approach significantly reduces query time while maintaining high explanation accuracy and outperforming baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness for scalable, interpretable data exploration.

title: Controlling Domain of Diffusion Models by Applying Product of Experts
authors: Shota Ino, Shugo Yamashita, Hideyuki Ogura, Kei Shibasaki, Masaaki Ikehara
publish: 2025 International Conference on Visual Communications and Image Processing (VCIP)
abstract: We propose PoE-Diffusion, a framework that incorporates the Product of Experts (PoE) into diffusion models. PoE-Diffusion enables sampling from a weighted product of probability distributions across multiple diffusion models without additional training. As an application of PoE-Diffusion, we introduce Pseudo-finetuning and evaluate it through experiments. We demonstrate that Pseudo-finetuning generates high-quality images with short training time, supported by both qualitative and quantitative results. PoE-Diffusion can be applied to any existing diffusion models as it only affects the inference process. This approach has the potential to be applied to a wide range of tasks that using diffusion model.

title: IntelliSense: A Vision-Driven Sensor Framework with Modeling for Indoor CO Emission Detection
authors: Shraddha Sahu, Ujjwal Biswas, Pradeepkumar Bhale
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th International Conference on Information and Communication Technology (CICT)
abstract: Carbon monoxide (CO) emissions pose significant health risks in indoor environments, particularly when originating from visually indistinct sources such as tobacco products and incense. This paper proposes a multi-modal system for real-time source attribution of CO emissions by integrating electrochemical sensing with deep convolutional object detection. A calibrated MQ-7 sensor array captures spatiotemporal variations in CO concentration, while a YOLOv8 model classifies co-occurring visual stimuli indicative of emission sources. Unlike traditional detectors, our system localizes emissions to specific source categories, enhancing interpretability and response strategies. Experiments conducted in controlled environments with dual-source emissions (cigarette, agarbatti) validate our model’s robustness across heterogeneous lighting and background conditions. The proposed pipeline achieves a maximum precision of 88.8% and a mean mAP@50 of 0.882 under 200-epoch training, with peak performance at epoch 75. The proposed framework provides a scalable baseline for future research on sensor–vision fusion in indoor air quality monitoring and smart city applications.

title: Piecing It Together: A Unified Diffusion Framework for Jigsaw Puzzle Reconstruction
authors: Vlassis Fotis, Ioannis Romanelis, Konstantinos Moustakas
publish: 2025 International Conference on Visual Communications and Image Processing (VCIP)
abstract: Solving jigsaw puzzles, a long-standing challenge in both human cognition and artificial intelligence, has seen significant progress with modern computer vision techniques. In this paper, we introduce a diffusion-based framework for jigsaw puzzle reconstruction, leveraging denoising diffusion models to iteratively refine piece placements. Unlike prior methods that rely on anchored reference pieces and relative positioning, our approach directly regresses absolute positions, making it more flexible and generalizable. Additionally, we extend puzzle-solving beyond square pieces by incorporating polygonal partitions and employ DDIM for efficient inference. Our modular pipeline is adaptable to various puzzle formulations, and we demonstrate its effectiveness by achieving state-of-the-art performance on the JPwLEG benchmark.

title: Convg: Graph-Manifold Learning of SPD Covariance for RSVP-Based EEG Target Detection
authors: Chao Chen, Yanan Zhao, Dongyue Wu, Dengpei Ji
publish: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Computer, Vision and Intelligent Technology (ICCVIT)
abstract: The Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm, known for its efficiency in visual stimulation, has been applied to tasks such as image interpretation and largescale image screening. By eliciting P300 event-related potentials (ERPs), RSVP aims to achieve rapid detection of relevant images within high-speed visual streams. However, accurately identifying target images remains a significant challenge due to the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and imbalance of sample classes. To address these challenges, we propose a model named ConvG which integrates graph structure modeling with manifold learning. Specifically, ConvG first employs the symmetric positive definite (SPD) covariance matrix as core representation for the time-frequency graph structure, which is followed by nonlinear feature extraction via GraphBiMap, ReEig, and LogEig operations. A focal loss strategy is subsequently introduced to mitigate performance degradation caused by target class imbalance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ConvG significantly improves the classification accuracy and robustness of object detection compared to existing methods on a selfconstructed RSVP dataset, validating its effectiveness in practical applications.

title: A Multi-Stage Deep Learning Approach for Alzheimer's Disease Classification Using MRI
authors: Vinukonda Emanuel Raju, B.N. Jagadesh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a degenerative neurological disorder and the leading cause of dementia worldwide. Prompt and accurate diagnosis is essential for effective therapy and enhanced patient outcomes. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides valuable non-invasive biomarkers of disease progression, but manual interpretation is prone to subjectivity and limited sensitivity. The paper presents a comprehensive deep learning system that integrates preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification to accurately stage Alzheimer's disease. The Open Access Series of Imaging Studies (OASIS) dataset, comprising 9,488 MRI scans across four classes (Non-Demented, Very Mild Dementia, Mild Dementia, and Moderate Dementia), was employed for experimentation. A U-Net-based segmentation pipeline, enhanced with pseudo-mask generation, was first applied to isolate brain regions from background artifacts. DenseNet121, pre-trained on ImageNet, was then used for feature extraction, generating 1024-dimensional embeddings. A ResNet-based multi-layer perceptron classifier incorporating dropout regularization was employed for multi-class classification, attaining precision, recall, and F1-scores surpassing 0.96 in all categories. The suggested framework achieved an overall accuracy of 99.3%, surpassing current CNN, LSTM, and attention-based models. The proposed method demonstrated superior robustness, particularly in distinguishing adjacent dementia stages. These findings confirm the clinical potential of segmentation-guided deep learning frameworks for reliable and scalable AD diagnosis.

title: TTRS: A Deep Learning Framework for Temple Time Recommendation and Crowd Optimization
authors: S. Abarna, J. Relin Francis Raj, R. Santhana Krishnan, R. Rajasree, N. Soundiraraj, A. Benazir Begum
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS)
abstract: The Temple Time Recommendation System (TTRS) is a comprehensive deep learning framework designed to forecast visitor footfall and recommend optimal temple visit times for improved crowd management and visitor satisfaction. The system combines the strengths of the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) for multi-horizon time series forecasting with a GRU-based Slot Scorer for personalized time-slot recommendation. The framework integrates diverse contextual data sources, including temporal attributes, event calendars, weather information, transport availability, sensor data, and user profiles, to generate accurate and context-aware predictions. The TFT captures both short-term and long-term dependencies in visitor patterns, while the GRU network models user-specific preferences to suggest time slots that minimize congestion and respect operational constraints. Explainable artificial intelligence methods, such as SHAP value analysis and attention visualization, were applied to enhance transparency and interpretability, allowing temple administrators to understand the key drivers of footfall variation and slot recommendations. Offline simulations demonstrated that TTRS effectively reduced peak-hour congestion by 20 percent, improved load balance by 30 percent, and achieved more than 85 percent user acceptance. The proposed framework represents a scalable and interpretable application of deep learning for real-world cultural and religious crowd management, establishing a foundation for future adaptive and context-aware scheduling systems.

title: A 2.4 GHz Down-Conversion Mixer with improved Figure of Merit for WLAN Applications
authors: Aryan Shrivastava, Swarnim Vinod Nirmal, Prasanna Kumar Misra, Pritesh Kumar Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th International Conference on Information and Communication Technology (CICT)
abstract: Enhanced Linearity proves to be an appropriate solution for the improvement of overall performance of RF circuit blocks in a receiver Front-end. In this work, a 2.4-GHz down-conversion Mixer with improved consolidated performance estimated via Figure of Merit (FOM) has been proposed in 180 nm CMOS process for WLAN applications. The improvement techniques include the combination of an inductor at the sources of switching stage with resistive source degeneration optimized around the sweet point. The overall trade-offs among the various RF performance parameters of the proposed down-conversion mixer are collectively quantified using a Figure of Merit (FOM) term. The proposed design demonstrates a conversion gain of 7.4 dB and a noise figure of 6.87 dB, while achieving a 1-dB compression point of −10.8 dBm and an input third-order intercept point of +0.194 dBm. With a power consumption of 20.7 mW, the resulting figure of merit reaches 7.5 at an IF frequency of 100 MHz. The mixer has been engineered to operate with a +1.8 V power supply, considering an RF input signal of -50 dBm and a Local Oscillator (LO) signal of +5 dBm. The enhanced linearity and overall performance render it a viable option for 2.4 GHz WLAN or sub-6 GHz applications.

title: Assurance Case Development for Evolving Software Product Lines: A Formal Approach
authors: Logan Murphy, Torin Viger, Alessio Di Sandro, Aren Babikian, Marsha Chechik
abstract: None

title: Exploring Digital-Age Work Behavior: How Cafe Facilities, Environment, and Location Influence the Rise of Work-from-Cafe Practices
authors: Fauzan Fatwa Amarullah, Nur Azmi Karim
publish: 2025 International Conference on ICT for Smart Society (ICISS)
abstract: This study aims to examine the influence of quality of facilities, environmental ambience, and location accessibility on the intention to work from cafés among flexible workers in Indonesia. A quantitative method using multiple linear regression analysis was employed to analyze data obtained from 400 respondents through an online survey. The findings indicate that the three variables collectively exert a significant effect with a coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}$</tex>) of 0.4643. Among the predictors, quality of facilities emerged as the most influential factor, followed by environmental ambience and location accessibility. These results underscore the importance of productivity-enhancing amenities-such as stable Wi-Fi, power outlets, and comfortable workspaces-in determining café preferences as alternative work environments. The study offers practical insights for café managers to prioritize improvements in physical infrastructure, and theoretical implications for the development of the third-place productivity concept. However, the study is limited by its crosssectional design and focus on only three primary variables. Future research is encouraged to incorporate mediating variables and adopt a longitudinal approach.

title: Analyzing the Integration of AI in E-Commerce: A Comprehensive Study on Compatibility and Security Frameworks
authors: Jibril Putri Genius Hidayanti, Doni Purnama Alamsyah, Satrio Matin Utomo
publish: 2025 International Conference on ICT for Smart Society (ICISS)
abstract: AI integration in e-commerce supports user experience and market potential. It is important to understand AI integration in e-commerce with the aim of technology adoption. The importance of AI in adoption technology, so the purpose of this study focuses on the study of AI integration support in e-commerce adoption with the support of perceived compatibility and security from users. The study was conducted quantitatively by testing a model of e-commerce adoption. Questionnaires were distributed through an online form to ecommerce users with AI facilities, where 238 users filled out the questionnaire. Respondents were obtained from active users in Bandung City (Indonesia) as a research sample. Data from the questionnaire was processed and analyzed using statistical tools (SmartPLS). The results of the study found that perceived compatibility and perceived security of e-commerce were seen as increasing by users due to AI support on the platform. AI Integration is an embedded system that supports good interaction in technology adoption in e-commerce. The research study explains that e-commerce adoption can be supported directly through perceived compatibility and security assessed by users. This study provides an important contribution to understanding how the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into e-commerce platforms can improve user perceptions of system compatibility and security. These findings are useful for developers and e-commerce service providers in designing AIbased systems that can strengthen user trust and comfort.

title: A Multimodal Collaborative Perception Framework in Challenging Environments
authors: Hanwen Jiang, Shijun Zhou, Konglin Zhu, Artur Andrzejak, Yi Gong
publish: 2025 9th IEEE International Conference on Network Intelligence and Digital Content (IC-NIDC)
abstract: This paper proposes a multimodal cooperative perception framework for autonomous driving systems in challenging environments. The framework employs a bird's-eye view paradigm (BEV) to unify the fusion of LiDAR cameras and adopts a modular design that enables flexible sensor and encoder configurations across agents, addressing heterogeneous collaboration requirements. The core innovation is a multi-scale attention-foreground-aware feature fusion network, integrating multi-scale feature extraction, foreground awareness, and attention mechanisms to achieve precise perception while enhancing robustness against localization errors and communication delays. Experimental results on the OPV2V and DAIR-V2X-C datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior detection accuracy with significantly fewer model parameters than state-of-the-art methods, reducing deployment costs and training time. In case of localization errors or communication delays, the proposed framework exhibits robust stability and reliability, offering an effective solution for real-world deployment.

title: Enhanced YOLOv11 Framework for Real-Time Railway Track Fault Detection and Safety Optimization
authors: Chiyyavaram Sahithi, G. Sreenivasulu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS)
abstract: The rapid extension of railway transportation system has made railway infrastructure monitoring as the major concern in order to avoid accidents and for operational safety. Old methods that involved human observation, sometimes took a while and were expensive and error-prone. sensor-based methods were traditionally time-consuming and extremely costly, yet unpredictable. The advancement of computer vision and deep learning can be leveraged to enhance the accuracy and reliability of automated defect detection systems. For interpretability, the pair of YOLOv11 detection framework with Canny edge detection and line visualization techniques is proposed here. This solution is encapsulated as a web app through Gradio and supports real-time interaction and visualization of results. From the experimental results on a small and carefully prepared dataset, YOLOv11 has surpassed the older versions such as YOLOv8 as well as other traditional methods such as Faster R-CNN in accuracy and robustness. It is found that this method reduces false alarms to a very great extent, performs well in real-time to monitor the railway track and can be employed in the advanced transportation.

title: KG-Code: A Knowledge Graph Guided Pre-Training Approach for Code Representation
authors: Hanyu Zhang, Tomoji Kishi
abstract: Recent years, with the widespread usage of large code pre-trained model, the application of deep learning in Software Engineering (SE) tasks has made significant progress. To achieve better performance, it is important to capture the code features during pre-training process. The existing studies have made many attempts for this challenge, such as trying to introduce Syntax Tree (AST) or data flow graph into pre-training process. However, existing studies still have limitations in the comprehensiveness and simplicity of capturing code features. Knowledge graphs (KGs), which represent entities and their relationships in graph structures, have been extensively applied in intelligent systems for domain-specific tasks. Inspired by this, we attempted a knowledge graph guided code pre-training approach in this study, hoping that the codespecific knowledge graph could help code pre-trained model achieve better code understanding ability. In our approach, we first proposed a new type of code-specific knowledge graph which integrates syntactic, structural, and semantic information from a code fragment. Next, we extract the code feature triplets and code concept triplets from the code knowledge graph as part of input data. We have also designed a specialized pre-training task to enhance the pre-trained model’s code feature comprehension ability. To evaluate our approach, we trained a baseline pre-trained model (KG-Code) and validated its effectiveness on two code understanding downstream tasks: code summarization and code clone detection. The experimental results show that introducing knowledge graph as guidance in the pre-training process could efficiently improve the code understanding ability.

title: Research on the Operational Status Evaluation and Aging Mechanism Characteristics of GIS Basin Insulators
authors: Gang Chen, Minggang Li, Mingguang Yu, Mengqi Yu, Yixin Gao, Xiangyang Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on New Energy and Power Engineering (ICNEPE)
abstract: In order to solve the aging failure problem of outdoor GIS basin insulators and improve the power supply reliability of power grid, this study takes 220 kV outdoor GIS basin insulators with different operating years as the object, and combines experimental and theoretical analysis to carry out research. The aging characteristic parameters are extracted by mechanical, electrical and high and low temperature aging tests. Based on finite element simulation and multi-model, the electro-thermal combined aging loss model is constructed. The multi-physical field coupling and machine learning fusion method is used to propose the residual life evaluation method, and the state evaluation software with more than five evaluation factors and more than 90% validity is developed. The results show that the bending strength of the 19-year sample is 32.5% lower than that of the new sample, and the partial discharge is more than 50 pC. The aging characteristic parameters are significantly correlated with the operating life, and the residual life prediction error of the model is less than 8%. The results provide support for the condition-based maintenance of GIS basin insulators, and have both economic and social benefits.

title: Design of Passive Wireless 3D Force Sensor System Based on LC Resonators
authors: Xin Chen, Baoqing Nie, Donglin Wu
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Consumer Electronics and Computer Engineering (ICCECE)
abstract: To address the challenges in three-dimensional (3D) force tactile sensing, including complex wiring, inconvenient power delivery, and the limited portability of conventional LC resonance frequency measurement that relies on bulky instruments, this paper proposes and implements a passive wireless 3D force sensor based on LC resonance, together with a frequency-swept readout system. The sensor adopts a three-layer architecture consisting of a ferrite film on the top electrode, an intermediate Ecoflex elastomeric pillar layer, and four square LC resonant coils on the bottom layer with distinct resonant frequencies. Under external loading, the normal force varies the separation between the ferrite film and the coils, while the tangential force changes the effective facing area, thereby modifying the coils’ equivalent inductance and shifting the resonant frequencies. For portable readout, a detection circuit based on direct digital synthesis (DDS) frequency sweeping and phase detection is designed. By monitoring the resonant-frequency variations of the four coils via a dedicated hardware front-end and a host computer, the applied force direction can be determined. Experimental results show that, within 0–2 N, the normal sensitivity along the −Z direction is 0.19 MHz/N. The tangential sensitivities are 0.26, 0.24, 0.32, and 0.30 MHz/N along the +X, −X, +Y, and −Y directions, respectively.
